mechanism_name: Environmental light intensity and pupil size
commonsense_rule: Environmental light intensity ↑ -> pupil constricts ↓ (the pupil becomes smaller to reduce light intake)
factual_rule: Environmental light intensity ↑ -> pupil constricts ↓ (the pupil becomes smaller to reduce light intake)
flipped_rule: Environmental light intensity ↑ -> pupil dilates ↑ (the stronger the light, the larger the pupil becomes instead)
key_visual_cue: Relationship between the number of bulb rays (light-intensity indicator) and black pupil area, showing an inverse/proportional relation

frame_descriptions.panel_1: Around the bulb there is 1 very short yellow ray, indicating a very dim-light environment; the cat-eye pupil is a very small black vertical slit.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: Around the bulb there are 4 medium-length yellow rays, indicating medium lighting; the cat-eye pupil expands into a medium-sized black vertical oval.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: Around the bulb there are 7 relatively long yellow rays, indicating a relatively bright environment; the cat-eye pupil expands markedly into a very large black near-circle, almost filling the iris area.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if a human eye were placed under the same kind of setup and the light became stronger?

choices.A: The pupil would stay the same size.
choices.B: The pupil would get larger as the light becomes stronger.
choices.C: The pupil size would determine how strong the light is.
choices.D: The pupil would get smaller.

required_induction: In this world, stronger environmental light leads to a larger pupil.

mechanism_name: Threat level and hedgehog spine angle
commonsense_rule: Threat/danger level ↑ -> hedgehog back-spine upright angle ↑ (more upright spines indicate stronger defense)
factual_rule: Threat/danger level ↑ -> hedgehog back-spine upright angle ↑ (more upright spines indicate stronger defense)
flipped_rule: Threat/danger level ↑ -> hedgehog back-spine upright angle ↓ (the more dangerous it is, the flatter the spines lie instead)
key_visual_cue: Relationship between fox-silhouette size/distance label (threat level) and hedgehog back-spine upright angle
visual_context: The red fox silhouette in front indicates the threat source; a larger silhouette and a closer position mean a higher level of threat to the hedgehog.

frame_descriptions.panel_2: The fox silhouette is medium-sized, located about 6m in front of the hedgehog, labeled "medium"; the hedgehog body is slightly curled, the back spines are moderately upright, about 50°.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The fox silhouette is large and very close, located about 2m in front of the hedgehog, labeled "near"; the hedgehog body returns to a normal relaxed walking posture, the back spines lie flat against the back, about 18°.

choices.B: They would stay the same.
choices.C: They would make the dog seem nearer and more dangerous.
choices.D: They would lie flatter against the back as the threat becomes greater in this world.

partial_error.reason: It notices the object substitution but ignores that the same visual rule should transfer to a different threat source.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating spine angle as the cause of threat level.

That means this world follows the flipped rule: greater threat leads to flatter spines. Replacing the fox with another threat source does not change the underlying induced rule.
required_induction: In this world, increasing threat makes the hedgehog's back spines flatten rather than rise.

choices.C: It would jump lower as the temperature becomes warmer.
choices.D: It would jump higher.

required_induction: In this world, higher environmental temperature leads to lower jump height.

mechanism_name: Dissolved oxygen in water and fish swimming depth
commonsense_rule: Dissolved oxygen content in water ↑ -> fish swimming depth ↑ (when dissolved oxygen is sufficient, fish swim freely at greater depths)

visual_context: The bubble icon above the fish tank indicates the dissolved oxygen level in the water, not bubbles exhaled by the goldfish. The goldfish's vertical position in the tank represents swimming depth; the closer to the bottom, the deeper.
